---
title: Syllabus
---

:::: {.grid .course-details}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-6 .g-col-md-4}
### Course

- {{< fa graduation-cap >}} {{< var course.level >}}
- {{< fa laptop >}} {{< var course.modality >}}
- {{< fa location-dot >}} {{< var course.location >}}
- {{< fa building-columns >}} {{< var university.name >}}
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-6 .g-col-md-4}
### Instructor

- {{< fa chalkboard-user >}} **{{< var instructor.name >}}** *(set each term)*
- {{< fa pen-nib >}} Developed by {{< var developer.name >}}
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### This offering

Each time the course runs, the instructor sets contact details, office hours, and the term calendar. Check your section's landing page (e.g., Blackboard) for this term's specifics; this site is the authoritative source for **content and assessment design**.
:::

::::


## Course Description

**{{< var course.number >}}: {{< var course.title >}}** is a graduate-level course that develops practical competence in regression-based data analysis using R. It follows a **"specific to general" pedagogical approach**, beginning with simple linear regression---a model most students have encountered---and progressively extending to more complex frameworks.

### Learning Arc

We begin with **Simple Linear Regression** and expand to:

- **ANCOVA and ANOVA** --- regression with categorical predictors
- **Multiple Regression** --- adding continuous predictors and understanding confounding
- **Interactions and Nonlinear Relationships** --- capturing complexity in data
- **Model Building and Diagnostics** --- ensuring model validity
- **Logistic Regression** --- extending to binary and ordinal outcomes
- **Generalized Linear Models** --- the unifying framework

By the end of the course, you'll understand that familiar tests like the *t*-test and ANOVA are simply special cases of the regression model.


## Learning Objectives

By the end of this course, you will be able to:

1. **Specify** linear and generalized linear regression models with continuous and categorical predictors, interactions, and nonlinear terms
2. **Interpret** regression coefficients, confidence intervals, and test statistics in substantive and statistical terms
3. **Diagnose** violations of model assumptions and identify influential observations
4. **Build** parsimonious models using confirmatory and exploratory strategies
5. **Extend** regression to binary and ordinal outcomes via logistic and ordinal regression
6. **Implement** statistical analyses in R with reproducible, well-documented code
7. **Communicate** statistical findings clearly to diverse audiences


## Course Materials

All course materials are **open-access** and free. You will also need R, RStudio, and internet access.

### Textbooks and resources

| Material | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| {{< fa book-open >}} [**Lecture Notes**]({{< var books.lecture_notes >}}){target="_blank"} | Primary textbook: {{< var materials.chapters >}} chapters covering theory, math, and R code |
| {{< fa laptop-code >}} [**Lab Manual**]({{< var books.r_lab_book >}}){target="_blank"} | {{< var materials.labs >}} hands-on R tutorials for applied practice |
| {{< fa circle-question >}} [**Practice Quizzes**]({{< var books.practice_quizzes >}}){target="_blank"} | {{< var materials.quizzes >}} interactive, ungraded self-checks that mirror the graded Module Quizzes, with instant feedback |
| {{< fa clipboard-list >}} [**Project Guide**]({{< var books.project_guide >}}){target="_blank"} | Guidelines, worked examples, and rubrics for the {{< var materials.projects >}} course projects |
| {{< fa database >}} [**regdatasets**]({{< var books.regdatasets >}}){target="_blank"} | R package with {{< var materials.datasets >}} curated educational datasets |

### Software

- **R** (free) --- Download from [cran.r-project.org](https://cran.r-project.org/)
- **RStudio** (free) --- Download from [posit.co](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/)
- **Posit Cloud** --- Free browser-based RStudio at [posit.cloud](https://posit.cloud/) (no installation needed)

For installation help, see the [R Setup Guide](/resource/r-setup.qmd).


## Course Structure

This is a **fully asynchronous, self-paced online course** organized into **{{< var materials.sessions >}} sessions** (see the [Schedule](/schedule.qmd)). In each session you will:

1. {{< fa book-open >}} **Read** the Lecture Notes chapter(s)
2. {{< fa laptop-code >}} **Complete** the matching R Lab tutorial
3. {{< fa circle-question >}} **Self-check** with the interactive Practice Quiz
4. {{< fa pencil >}} **Submit** Homework, a graded **Module Quiz**, or a Project milestone (when due)

**Expected workload**: approximately 10--12 hours per week for a 3-credit graduate course.


## Grading and Assessment

::: {.callout-note}
## Grading philosophy

This course prioritizes **learning over perfection**. Homework is graded on completion (not correctness), and graded quizzes allow unlimited attempts. This approach reduces statistics anxiety and encourages genuine engagement with the material.

*This website is the authoritative source for the assessment design. If earlier course documents differ, follow the plan described here.*
:::

Your final grade is based on four components:

### Grading breakdown

::: {.assessment-donut}
<svg class="donut" viewBox="0 0 42 42" role="img" aria-label="Grade weighting: Homework 50 percent, Project 1 (Linear) 20 percent, Project 2 (Logistic) 20 percent, Module Quizzes 10 percent.">
  <circle cx="21" cy="21" r="15.915" fill="none" stroke="#e5e7eb" stroke-width="5"/>
  <circle cx="21" cy="21" r="15.915" fill="none" stroke="#0B3D66" stroke-width="5" stroke-dasharray="50 50" stroke-dashoffset="25"/>
  <circle cx="21" cy="21" r="15.915" fill="none" stroke="#3E8E8A" stroke-width="5" stroke-dasharray="20 80" stroke-dashoffset="-25"/>
  <circle cx="21" cy="21" r="15.915" fill="none" stroke="#A8431E" stroke-width="5" stroke-dasharray="20 80" stroke-dashoffset="-45"/>
  <circle cx="21" cy="21" r="15.915" fill="none" stroke="#C9A227" stroke-width="5" stroke-dasharray="10 90" stroke-dashoffset="-65"/>
  <text class="donut-hole-num" x="21" y="20.6" text-anchor="middle" font-size="6">100%</text>
  <text class="donut-hole-lbl" x="21" y="25" text-anchor="middle" font-size="2.6">of grade</text>
</svg>

<ul class="donut-legend">
<li><span class="sw" style="background:#0B3D66"></span> <strong>Homework</strong> — 50%</li>
<li><span class="sw" style="background:#3E8E8A"></span> <strong>Project 1</strong> · Linear — 20%</li>
<li><span class="sw" style="background:#A8431E"></span> <strong>Project 2</strong> · Logistic — 20%</li>
<li><span class="sw" style="background:#C9A227"></span> <strong>Module Quizzes</strong> — 10%</li>
</ul>
:::

| Category | Weight | Details |
|----------|:------:|---------|
| **Homework** | 50% | {{< var materials.homeworks >}} assignments (completion-based) |
| **Module Quizzes** | 10% | {{< var materials.quizzes >}} quizzes (mastery-based, unlimited attempts) |
| **Project 1: Linear Regression** | 20% | Proposal + Draft + Final Report |
| **Project 2: Logistic Regression** | 20% | Proposal + Draft + Final Report |
| **Total** | **100%** | |

### Assignment details

**Homework (50%)**: {{< var materials.homeworks >}} assignments, roughly one every other session. They alternate between **conceptual** problems (hand calculations, interpretation) and **computational** problems (R code, data analysis). Graded for **completion** --- full credit for a genuine, meaningful attempt; zero for non-submission. A meaningful attempt means:

- All problems are attempted (even if you are unsure of the answer)
- For computational work: your R code runs without errors
- Written interpretations are included where requested

**Module Quizzes (10%)**: {{< var materials.quizzes >}} concept-check quizzes (multiple-choice, short-answer, and more) delivered on Blackboard. **Mastery-based**: unlimited attempts, highest score counts, open-book and open-notes; scoring 80% or higher earns full credit. Each graded quiz has a matching **[interactive Practice Quiz]({{< var books.practice_quizzes >}}){target="_blank"}** --- ungraded, unlimited, with instant per-question feedback and deep links into the Lecture Notes --- use it to prepare.

**Project 1: Linear Regression (20%)**: A complete linear regression analysis on a dataset of your choosing.

| Component | Due | Weight |
|-----------|:---:|:------:|
| Proposal | Session 7 | 30% of Project 1 |
| Draft | Session 9 | 30% of Project 1 |
| Final Report | Session 11 | 40% of Project 1 |

**Project 2: Logistic Regression (20%)**: A complete logistic regression analysis, building on the skills from Project 1.

| Component | Due | Weight |
|-----------|:---:|:------:|
| Proposal | Session 12 | 20% of Project 2 |
| Draft | Session 14 | 30% of Project 2 |
| Final Report | Session 15 | 50% of Project 2 |

See the [Project Guide]({{< var books.project_guide >}}){target="_blank"} for detailed guidelines and worked examples.

### Letter grades

| Grade | Range | | Grade | Range |
|:-----:|:-----:|-|:-----:|:-----:|
| A | 90--100% | | C | 70--76% |
| B+ | 87--89% | | D | 60--69% |
| B | 80--86% | | F | < 60% |
| C+ | 77--79% | | | |


## Course Policies

### Late work

- **Project milestones**: **10% per day** deducted for late submissions; absolute cutoff **7 days late** without prior arrangement.
- **Homework** is completion-graded --- submit each assignment by its session to earn credit. If you fall behind, contact your instructor; extensions are routinely granted for documented hardships.
- **Module Quizzes** allow unlimited attempts with no late penalty.

### Academic integrity

All work must comply with the [University of Alabama Academic Misconduct Policy](https://catalog.ua.edu/undergraduate/about/academic-regulations/student-expectations/academic-misconduct-policy/).

**Regarding software tools and outside assistance**:

- You **may** use software tools and reference materials to learn concepts and debug code
- You **must** understand, adapt, and be able to explain any code you submit
- You **cannot** submit work that you do not understand or that substantially comes from outside assistance without meaningful revision and attribution
- If in doubt, disclose outside assistance --- transparency is never penalized

### Disability accommodations

Students with disabilities should register with the [Office of Disability Services (ODS)](https://ods.ua.edu/) and share their accommodation letter early in the term.

### Religious observances

Notify your instructor at least one week in advance if religious observances conflict with deadlines.

### Title IX

The University of Alabama is committed to providing a safe environment. For confidential support, contact the [UA Counseling Center](https://counseling.sl.ua.edu/) at (205) 348-3863. For formal reports, contact the [Title IX Office](https://titleix.ua.edu/) at (205) 348-5496.


## Getting Help

::: {.callout-important}
## You can do this

Every programmer --- from beginners to experts --- gets stuck. It is 100% normal.

When you're stuck:

1. **Take a break.** Step away. Your subconscious will keep working.
2. **Read the error message carefully.** Search "rstats [your error]".
3. **Ask for help.** Reach out to your instructor or classmates.
4. **Review the Lab Manual.** Work through the examples step by step.

You will get unstuck.
:::

### Contacting your instructor

Your instructor posts their preferred contact method, response-time policy, and office hours on your section's landing page (e.g., Blackboard), along with term-specific announcements and deadlines.

### Additional support

- **Counseling Center**: [counseling.sl.ua.edu](https://counseling.sl.ua.edu/) --- free, confidential support
- **Dean of Students**: [dos.sl.ua.edu](https://dos.sl.ua.edu/) --- emergency financial assistance, food pantry, housing support
- **Writing Center**: [writingcenter.ua.edu](https://writingcenter.ua.edu/)
- **UA IT Help Desk**: [oit.ua.edu](https://oit.ua.edu/) --- (205) 348-5555

### Course resources

Browse the [Resources](/resource/index.qmd) page for R setup guides, dataset documentation, style guides, and FAQs.
